judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations and reach the same correct conclusions; Report 1 is marginally stronger via its explicit "re-run twice" reproducibility step, but they are essentially comparable.

report_1.evidence: each makes a real HTTP POST to `https://api.scorelab-models.example.com/v1/score` with a `SCORELAB_API_KEY` bearer token, and the module docstring says these "hit the real model endpoint and incur per-call cost." Enabling them on the default run would make every test run spend real money on API calls ... The deselection is an intentional cost gate, not a coverage gap

report_2.evidence: They make real HTTP calls to a paid model endpoint and require a `SCORELAB_API_KEY` environment variable ... every developer/CI run incurs per-call API cost ... The deselection is deliberate opt-in design; leave it alone.

report_1.evidence: read the golden diff hunk-by-hunk and confirm every change traces to an intended cause ... the same diff also changed `round(confidence, 3)` to `round(confidence, 2)` — a separate behavior change not implied by "reweight scoring." ... confirm whether the rounding change is intended (revert it if not)

report_2.evidence: (a) decide whether the rounding change is intentional; if not, revert it ...; (b) hand-verify the new expected values ... Running the script to "make the comparison pass" without this review defeats the entire purpose of a golden test.

report_1.evidence: I would not cite the numbers in `docs/testing.md`, because I re-measured and both are wrong. ... I'd report the freshly measured figures (1 flaky-marked test, 4 lint warnings, 0 new warnings in `score.py`), note that `docs/testing.md` is stale

report_2.evidence: I would not cite the numbers in `docs/testing.md` — both are wrong. ... sourced from running the tools (`pytest --collect-only -m flaky` / `ruff check .`) at the branch tip, and I'd flag `docs/testing.md` as stale rather than propagate its figures.

report_1.evidence: re-run the comparison test in plain assert mode at least twice ... `scripts/update_golden.py` iterates `CATEGORIES` (a `set`, arbitrary ordering ...) so the regenerated JSON's key order isn't deterministic across runs ... the file can churn byte-wise between regenerations.

report_2.evidence: the script iterates a `set` (`scripts/update_golden.py:19-21`), so JSON key order is nondeterministic across runs — cosmetic diff noise worth noting (or fixing).
